content:
First, note that since all a_i lie between 2.56 and 2.86, any “best‐fit’’ integers will be either 2 or 3 (rounding to 1 or 4 would incur an error ≥1.14, much worse than the answer we will find).

We need seven integers A_i∈{2,3} summing to 19.  If we let k be the number of 3’s, then

 sum A_i = 3·k + 2·(7−k) = 14 + k

must equal 19, so k = 5.  Hence exactly five of the A_i’s are 3 and two of them are 2.

Label the a_i in increasing order:

  a₁=2.56, a₂=2.61, a₃=2.65, a₄=2.71, a₅=2.79, a₆=2.82, a₇=2.86.

To minimize the maximum error M = max_i |A_i−a_i|, we should assign the two 2’s to the two smallest a_i (since |2−a| grows as a grows) and the five 3’s to the five largest a_i.  Concretely:

  A₁=2, A₂=2, A₃=A₄=A₅=A₆=A₇=3.

The resulting errors are

  |2−2.56|=0.56, |2−2.61|=0.61,  
  |3−2.65|=0.35, |3−2.71|=0.29, |3−2.79|=0.21, |3−2.82|=0.18, |3−2.86|=0.14,

so the maximum is M = 0.61.  One checks easily that no choice can make all errors ≤0.60 (you wouldn’t get enough 2’s or 3’s to hit sum 19), so 0.61 is minimal.

Therefore 100·M = 61, and the answer is

\boxed{61}.

  a₁=2.56, a₂=2.61, a₃=2.65, a₄=2.71, a₅=2.79, a₆=2.82, a₇=2.86.

To minimize the maximum error M = max_i |A_i−a_i|, we should assign the two 2’s to the two smallest a_i (since |2−a| grows as a grows) and the five 3’s to the five largest a_i.  Concretely:

  A₁=2, A₂=2, A₃=A₄=A₅=A₆=A₇=3.

The resulting errors are

so the maximum is M = 0.61.  One checks easily that no choice can make all errors ≤0.60 (you wouldn’t get enough 2’s or 3’s to hit sum 19), so 0.61 is minimal.

Therefore 100·M = 61, and the answer is

\boxed{61}.
